stimulus:
Text 1

Video games have grown into a multibillion-dollar industry, but they shouldn’t be considered a form of storytelling. Storytelling, by definition, conveys a fixed narrative through which a creator’s vision is communicated to an audience; in video games, however, the player’s choices determine what happens, so any resulting “story” is largely accidental. Games are better understood as toys: structured systems built to be played with rather than narratives meant to be received.

 

Text 2

The interactive elements of video games are precisely what allow them to tell stories impossible in other media. A designer arranges environments, characters, and consequences so that the player’s decisions illuminate themes the designer has carefully prepared in advance. Games such as Disco Elysium and What Remains of Edith Finch deliver narratives whose meaning depends on the player’s participation, not on the absence of authorial intent.
stem: Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to the overall argument presented in Text 1?

answerOptions.content: By conceding that the narratives produced through gameplay are too unpredictable to be considered the work of any single creator
By suggesting that the storylines of certain video games are far more difficult to follow than the author of Text 1 acknowledges
By contending that the player’s decisions in video games operate within structures deliberately shaped by a designer to convey meaning
By agreeing with the author of Text 1 that most video games are less artistically ambitious than most works of traditional fiction

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer because it most accurately states how the author of Text 2 would respond to the argument in Text 1. Text 1 claims that the player’s choices make any resulting story accidental, so games cannot count as storytelling. Text 2 directly counters this, arguing that designers “arrange environments, characters, and consequences” to ensure that the player’s decisions illuminate themes “carefully prepared in advance.” The author of Text 2 would therefore say that interactivity does not eliminate authorial intent but channels it.

Choice A is incorrect because Text 2 explicitly attributes a single authorial vision (the designer’s) to the narrative. Choice B is incorrect because neither text addresses the difficulty of following storylines. Choice D is incorrect because Text 2 highlights games that achieve sophisticated narratives, not games that fall short of traditional fiction.
